primary_diagnosis.name: Superficial thrombophlebitis complicating pre-existing lower-limb varicose veins (chronic venous insufficiency)

comorbidities.name: Varicose veins of the lower extremities with chronic venous insufficiency

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic venous insufficiency from incompetent superficial venous valves produces venous hypertension, dilated tortuous varicosities, dependent aching relieved by elevation, and skin changes (venous eczema, haemosiderin staining). Stasis within a varicosity predisposes to localized thrombosis and inflammation of the superficial vein wall — superficial thrombophlebitis — giving the classic tender, firm, erythematous palpable cord following the course of the vein. Superficial thrombophlebitis is usually benign and self-limiting, but is important not to miss because (1) a concurrent deep vein thrombosis coexists in a clinically meaningful minority and cannot be excluded on inspection alone, and (2) extensive thrombophlebitis or thrombus approaching the saphenofemoral or saphenopopliteal junction can propagate into the deep system and embolise. Definitive assessment of clot extent and exclusion of DVT requires venous duplex ultrasound and hands-on examination — neither available via telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: A new tender, firm, red cord along a known varicosity is the classic presentation.
Must be actively excluded — coexists with superficial thrombophlebitis in a meaningful minority and is limb/life-threatening; cannot be excluded remotely.
Red, warm, tender skin can mimic cellulitis; distinguished by linear erythema tracking a vein and absence of fever/spreading plaque.
Localized cord over a varicosity, afebrile, no massive calf swelling or deep calf pain — fits superficial thrombophlebitis.
Absence of whole-calf swelling and deep calf pain lowers probability, but DVT cannot be clinically excluded without examination and duplex — remains a must-exclude in a telehealth encounter.
Afebrile with linear erythema following the vein argues against cellulitis but does not fully exclude it.
Symptoms are relieved by elevation (venous pattern), not worsened by it (arterial pattern); no rest pain or claudication described.

Classic constellation; benign in-person diagnosis in the source, but telehealth cannot exclude concurrent DVT or map clot extent — urgent same-day in-person assessment plus duplex is required before reassurance.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth-correct endpoint is a presumptive diagnosis with mandatory urgent in-person assessment to exclude DVT and assess proximity to the saphenofemoral junction — NOT a confident benign sign-off.

red_flags.description: New tender, firm, erythematous cord along a varicosity — superficial thrombophlebitis that can propagate toward the saphenofemoral junction and into the deep venous system
red_flags.clinical_significance: Extension of superficial thrombus into the deep system risks DVT and pulmonary embolism; extent and junctional proximity must be assessed by duplex.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Falsely reassuring a propagating thrombophlebitis can delay anticoagulation and allow deep extension or embolism.

red_flags.description: Concurrent deep vein thrombosis cannot be excluded via telehealth (no palpation, no calf-circumference comparison, no duplex)
red_flags.clinical_significance: DVT coexists with superficial thrombophlebitis in a clinically meaningful minority and is limb/life-threatening; remote exclusion is impossible.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed DVT can progress to pulmonary embolism.

red_flags.description: Massive unilateral whole-calf swelling — screened negative in this encounter
red_flags.clinical_significance: Its presence would sharply raise DVT probability and mandate emergency assessment.

red_flags.description: Chest pain / breathlessness / haemoptysis suggesting pulmonary embolism — screened negative in this encounter
red_flags.clinical_significance: Development of these features is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate 000.

red_flags.description: Fever or rapidly spreading erythema suggesting cellulitis / systemic infection — screened negative in this encounter
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features or spreading redness would shift management toward antibiotics and urgent review.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the known 'varicose veins' and dismissing a new hard red cord as harmless, missing that superficial thrombophlebitis can extend and that DVT cannot be excluded remotely.
Assuming a localized cord rules out DVT — the two coexist, and calf-circumference, palpation and duplex are all unavailable over chat.
Treating linear erythema over a vein in an afebrile patient as cellulitis and prescribing antibiotics unnecessarily.
Starting anticoagulation remotely before duplex defines clot extent and proximity to the saphenofemoral junction — a decision that belongs after imaging.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the cord to confirm it is a thrombosed superficial vein and gauge its length/warmth
Calf-circumference comparison between legs to assess for DVT
Homan's sign and deep calf tenderness
Palpation of pedal pulses to exclude arterial disease
Venous duplex ultrasound to confirm superficial thrombosis, map its extent, and exclude deep vein thrombosis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a new symptomatic superficial thrombophlebitis on a background of varicose veins and arrange an URGENT same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care) that can examine the leg and obtain a venous duplex to exclude DVT and assess junctional proximity; provide interim symptomatic advice and clear escalation triggers. Escalate to ED/000 immediately if PE features, massive calf swelling, or rapidly spreading infection develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The real risk is UNDER-action — reassuring the patient that varicose veins are benign and deferring to routine review, thereby missing a concurrent or propagating DVT that telehealth cannot exclude.
